lines: on absence -- {bust_handbook_windows} scope (install + Run-Unattended + first auth)
+. *Assert sshd_config Match block routes workload to absolute-path authkeys*:
+hardening). Workload SSH auth depends on this block: without it, sshd
+falls back to the workload's profile-relative `authorized_keys` path and
+exposes the demotion failure mode that absolute-path Match defends against.
+1). {busn_garrison} on `w` writes the bare workload authkeys file into
+this directory and the lockdown rewrite overwrites it; absence here
+. *Assert workload authkeys directory ACL = admins+SYSTEM only*:
+as Full-Control principals AND contains none of `BUILTIN\Users`,
+AUTHORITY\SYSTEM and does not need workload-side grants; parallel to
+on missing required principal or forbidden principal present --

input.params: {"identity": "A-ABG", "files": ["Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh", "Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJIW-InvigilateWindows.adoc", "Tools/buk/vov_veiled/WSG-WindowsScriptingGuide.md"], "intent": "Close two BUSJIW/transport-helper gaps surfaced by the bujn-winpc field exercise of garrison-w: a silent obliterate-corruption from a missing WSG escape invariant in PowerShell wrapper helpers, and a missing-fact gap in invigilate-windows that let garrison-w trip past preflight onto a fatal place-bare-trust DirectoryNotFoundException. WSG: mint ✅ WSp-109 'Wrapper helpers escape body \" unconditionally' between ❌ WSp-108 and ❌ WSs-101 — names the helper-author invariant (any bash helper that wraps caller-provided ${z_body} inside outer \"...\" argv quoting MUST apply ${z_body//\\\"/\\\\\\\"} before interpolation), distinguishes helper-author obligation from body-author burden (body authors stay free to write inner \" literally), cross-refs WSt-102 as the underlying mechanism, names the failure signature 'A positional parameter cannot be found that accepts argument'<token>'' that surfaced empirically on the Phase 2 Win32_UserProfile probe, includes ❌/✅ paired worked example showing the transform inline at the helper site, and scopes the rule to wrappers that compose outer \"...\" (thin ssh '$user@$host' '$cmd' passthroughs are out of scope — no inner-quote collision to defend against). Wrapper Discipline section: all three template wrappers (PowerShell, bash-via-wsl, bash-via-cygwin) updated to show 'local -r z_body_escaped=\"${z_body//\\\"/\\\\\\\"}\" # WSp-109' as the first line and interpolate ${z_body_escaped} thereafter, with a leading single-line note that 'all three wrappers compose outer \"...\" around ${z_body} and so apply the WSp-109 escape transform unconditionally'. bujb_jurisdiction.sh: zbujb_admin_powershell and zbujb_powershell_capture each gain the z_body_escaped transform line right after the z_body local, with the # WSp-109 anchor comment; the interpolated reference at the SSH-arg-construction line switches from ${z_body} to ${z_body_escaped}. Pre-existing zbujb_admin_exec_* (line 545) already had the transform via a literal ${z_body//\\\"/\\\\\\\"} substitution — the new helpers parallel its shape exactly. Failure mode that triggered the fix: garrison-w obliterate Phase 2 probe body 'Get-CimInstance -ClassName Win32_UserProfile -Filter \"LocalPath = ... OR LocalPath LIKE ...\"' contained inner \" chars; pre-fix the outer \"...\" wrapping made cmd.exe terminate the quoted string at the first inner \", PS saw 'LocalPath' as the -Filter value and '=', '...' as positional args, dispatch returned non-zero; bash absorbed the failure via '|| z_profiles_raw=\"\"' (legitimate per WSG capture absorption when absent-state is a valid pre-condition); Phase 2 dispatch loop iterated zero rows; Win32_UserProfile orphans and HKLM ProfileList SID entries pointing at .NNN demotion paths survived obliterate silently. Post-fix the transform produces 'Get-CimInstance ... -Filter \\\"LocalPath = ... OR LocalPath LIKE ...\\\"' on the wire; cmd.exe unescapes \\\" to \" reaching PS; PS sees the Filter value correctly; rows return; dispatch iterates per-row Remove-CimInstance. Phase 2 dispatch (line 835) inner-\" body Remove-CimInstance -Query \"SELECT ...\" was also affected by the same bug but never fired pre-fix because the probe returned empty; post-fix both probe and dispatch are correct. zbujb_caparison_windows_verify_match_block: helper die messages extended with '— caparison-windows (BUSJCW)' failure pointer suffix on all four buc_die sites (empty bytes, missing header, header-present-no-AKF, AKF mismatch) so the helper is reusable from invigilate without losing the operator's pointer-to-fix. Comment block extended to note the dual-use (caparison post-completion verify + invigilate full audit). Existing caparison post-restart verify call at line 1759 unchanged — its existing buc_die context still reads correctly with the new appended suffix. zbujb_invigilate_windows_caparison_facts: three new facts added before the existing four (thematic order: SSH-trust posture from BUSJCW Phase 1 precedes post-trust Phase 3 deliverables): Fact A 'sshd_config Match User ${BUJB_workload_user} routes AuthorizedKeysFile to absolute path' (Get-Content sshd_config -Raw via zbujb_admin_powershell, then zbujb_caparison_windows_verify_match_block does the bash-side parse — header line exact-match + next-non-blank stripped-indent match against 'AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys'), Fact B 'workload authkeys directory present at $env:ProgramData\\\\ssh\\\\users\\\\${BUJB_workload_user}' (Test-Path via zbujb_powershell_capture; require equals True; fail on False or transport error), Fact C 'workload authkeys directory ACL = admins+SYSTEM Full Control only' (icacls via zbujb_admin_powershell, bash-side parse strips 'Successfully processed' trailer, asserts both BUILTIN\\\\Administrators and NT AUTHORITY\\\\SYSTEM principals present AND none of BUILTIN\\\\Users / Authenticated Users / ${BUJB_workload_user} present — the third forbidden principal catches the workload-Read grant variant that the operator's prior Q3=clean decision retired with the note that sshd reads as NT AUTHORITY\\\\SYSTEM and does not need workload-side grants, parallel to administrators_authorized_keys ACL discipline). Function header comment updated to enumerate the new shape (Phase 1 SSH-trust posture + Phase 3 post-trust admin posture) and clarify the dual-invocation context (caparison post-completion verifies its own work; invigilate full audit verifies state). The three new facts are caparison-scope (failure pointer BUSJCW); they do not affect the operator-handbook facts in zbujb_invigilate_windows_op_facts (which remain BUSJHW-pointered). BUSJIW spec: three new axhos_step entries inserted after the operator-managed registry/AoAc/Tailscale-registered cluster and before the existing Tailscale-StartType-onward caparison cluster, each carrying {busc_call}/{busc_store}/{busc_require}/{busc_fatal} markers per orchestration-spec discipline. Step A names the «SSHD_CONFIG_RAW» variable produced by Get-Content sshd_config -Raw, requires the canonical Match User block shape with indentation-tolerant AKF directive, anchors the failure to BUSJCW Phase 1 SSH-trust hardening, and explicates the demotion-defense rationale inline (without the absolute-path Match, sshd falls back to workload-profile-relative authkeys exposing the demotion failure mode the absolute path defends against). Step B names «AUTHKEYS_DIR_PRESENT», requires equals True, anchors to BUSJCW Phase 1, and names the load-bearing consequence inline (garrison-w place-bare-trust writes into this directory and fails with DirectoryNotFoundException if absent — the exact failure that triggered this slice). Step C names «AUTHKEYS_DIR_ACL», requires both admins+SYSTEM principals present AND none of Users/Authenticated Users/${BUJB_workload_user} present, anchors to BUSJCW Phase 1 with the icacls invocation reproduced inline. Spec changes coherent with the bash implementation order. Gates: bash -n on bujb_jurisdiction.sh clean; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh 5 fixtures / 28 cases pass; tt/rbtd-r.FixtureRun.handbook-render.sh 15/15; tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh 4 fixtures (enrollment-validation 47, regime-validation 27, regime-smoke 9, handbook-render 15) pass. Field exercise on bujn-winpc deferred to immediate follow-on caparison-windows run in the same chat (operator amendment 'notch before running caparison'). Pre-existing operator manual cleanup completed in-chat ahead of this notch: C:\\\\WSL orphan swept via privileged SSH (wsl --shutdown + wsl --unregister rbtww-main + Remove-Item -Recurse -Force C:\\\\WSL), validating the destructive-cmdlet privileged-SSH transport before relying on it in caparison-windows. No BCG changes — the bash-side capture/probe discipline lives in WSG's Capture-Decide-Dispatch section (already correct); the bug was a WSG-domain wrapper-author invariant gap, not a BCG-domain bash-pattern gap. Pace-affiliated commit per the pace docket's interleaved-spec-and-bash sanction; closes two of the operator's surfaced field-exercise items (BUSJIW fact gap + WSG wrapper-helper escape invariant). Open after this notch: re-run caparison-windows on bujn-winpc to validate the new directory-provisioning + Match block + ACL deliverables land cleanly, then re-run garrison-w to validate the post-caparison preflight passes the three new facts and the Phase 2 obliterate probe now returns rows correctly under the WSp-109 fix."}

PS C:\Users\bhyslop> $ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> 
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> $pubkey = 'ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAINqo5TftE69ZojgSucBTbNJujahF0yCkxbKxJ6AR9aZJ bhyslop@winpc-admin'
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> $adminAuthKeys = "$env:ProgramData\ssh\administrators_authorized_keys"
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> $sshConfig    = "$env:ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config"
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> 
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> # Idempotent admin pubkey install
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> if (-not (Test-Path $adminAuthKeys)) {
>>   New-Item -Path $adminAuthKeys -ItemType File -Force | Out-Null
>> }
>> $existingLines = @(Get-Content $adminAuthKeys -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)
>> if ($existingLines -notcontains $pubkey) {
>>   Add-Content -Path $adminAuthKeys -Value $pubkey -Encoding ASCII
>> }
>> 
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> # icacls lockdown (idempotent)
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> icacls $adminAuthKeys /inheritance:r /grant 'SYSTEM:F' /grant 'BUILTIN\Administrators:F' 2>&1 | Out-Null
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { throw "icacls failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)" }
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> 
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> # Idempotent sshd_config harden �?" replace the first matching line for
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> # each directive (commented or otherwise) and drop subsequent duplicates;
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> # append directives that are absent. Convergent on re-run.
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> $directives = [ordered]@{
>>   'PubkeyAuthentication' = 'yes'
>>   'PasswordAuthentication' = 'no'
>>   'PermitEmptyPasswords' = 'no'
>> }
>> 
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> $lines = @(Get-Content $sshConfig)
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> $out   = New-Object System.Collections.Generic.List[string]
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> $seen  = @{}
PS C:\Users\bhyslop> foreach ($line in $lines) {
>>   $matched = $false
>>   foreach ($k in $directives.Keys) {
>>     if ($line -match "^\s*#*\s*$k\s+\S") {
>>       if (-not $seen.ContainsKey($k)) {
>>         $out.Add("$k $($directives[$k])")
>>         $seen[$k] = $true
>>       }
>>       $matched = $true
>>       break
>>     }
>>   }
>>   if (-not $matched) { $out.Add($line) }
>> }
>> foreach ($k in $directives.Keys) {
>>   if (-not $seen.ContainsKey($k)) { $out.Add("$k $($directives[$k])") }
>> }
>> Set-Content -Path $sshConfig -Value $out -Encoding ASCII
>>

PS C:\Users\bhyslop> if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { throw "sshd -t failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)" }

# This is the sshd server system-wide configuration file.  See
# sshd_config(5) for more information.

# The strategy used for options in the default sshd_config shipped with

# default value.

#Port 22
#AddressFamily any
#ListenAddress 0.0.0.0
#ListenAddress ::

#HostKey __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/ssh_host_rsa_key
#HostKey __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/ssh_host_dsa_key
#HostKey __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key
#HostKey __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/ssh_host_ed25519_key

# Ciphers and keying
#RekeyLimit default none

# Logging
#SyslogFacility AUTH
#LogLevel INFO

# Authentication:

#LoginGraceTime 2m
#PermitRootLogin prohibit-password
#StrictModes yes
#MaxAuthTries 6
#MaxSessions 10

PubkeyAuthentication yes

# The default is to check both .ssh/authorized_keys and .ssh/authorized_keys2
# but this is overridden so installations will only check .ssh/authorized_keys
AuthorizedKeysFile	.ssh/authorized_keys

#AuthorizedPrincipalsFile none

#HostbasedAuthentication no
# Change to yes if you don't trust ~/.ssh/known_hosts for

#IgnoreUserKnownHosts no

#IgnoreRhosts yes

PasswordAuthentication no
PermitEmptyPasswords no

# GSSAPI options

#AllowAgentForwarding yes
#AllowTcpForwarding yes
#GatewayPorts no
#PermitTTY yes

#ClientAliveInterval 0

# no default banner path

# override default of no subsystems

# Example of overriding settings on a per-user basis

#	AllowTcpForwarding no
#	PermitTTY no
#	ForceCommand cvs server

LogLevel DEBUG3
Match Group administrators
       AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/administrators_authorized_keys

content.text:
A new bug surfaced — and a more dangerous one than the original. Stopping before any further edits.

## Diagnosis: chunkA stdout is a PowerShell REPL transcript, not raw sshd_config bytes

The caparison-windows chunk-A SSH session feeds its multi-line script via heredoc through `zbujb_caparison_windows_exec_with_password_fallback`. **The interactive PowerShell session echoes every input line back as `PS C:\Users\bhyslop> <line>` and `>> <continuation>`** — those echoes get captured into chunkA_stdout alongside the actual file content that the trailing `Get-Content $sshConfig -Raw` produced.

The chunkA_stdout transcript (see `bujb_caparison_100_phase1_chunkA_stdout.txt`):
- Lines 1-56: PS REPL transcript of the script being executed (prompts + script source echoed)
- Lines 57-148: actual sshd_config raw bytes (the legitimate output of the trailing Get-Content)
- Line 149: trailing `PS C:\Users\bhyslop>` prompt

**Before a69f25fb:** chunk-A's stdout was only consumed by the directives-verify parse, which scans for specific lines like `PubkeyAuthentication yes` — that parse tolerates the REPL bloat because it grep-style matches expected directives and ignores everything else.

**After a69f25fb:** the new Match block append code takes chunkA_stdout bytes, strips any prior `Match User <workload>` block, appends the canonical block, base64-encodes, and writes via `WriteAllBytes`. The strip+append logic doesn't know about REPL prompts, so the entire transcript gets re-written to sshd_config as if it were config syntax.

**Current state of bujn-winpc:**
- `sshd_config` on disk is malformed (REPL transcript prepended + Match block appended)
- `sshd -t` (in the new Phase 1 re-validate step) failed and aborted before chunk-B's Restart-Service
- Running sshd still serves the OLD config (no Match block, no bloat) because the service never reloaded
- New SSH connections still work because sshd holds the old config in memory
- **But a service restart or OS reboot will brick admin SSH** — sshd will fail to come up with the malformed config

## Why the missing stderr on `sshd -t` failure

Two contributing factors:
1. The sshd -t re-validate uses `zbujb_admin_powershell` (non-interactive `powershell -Command`), which doesn't capture sshd.exe's stderr the way the REPL would. PowerShell's `$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'` throws on non-zero exit but the sshd.exe stderr stream may go to a different FD that doesn't propagate cleanly through the cmd.exe / SSH transport.
2. The bash side captures stderr into `bujb_caparison_104_p1-sshd-t-rev_stderr.txt` (0 bytes) — meaning whatever sshd.exe wrote didn't make it back. This is a separate diagnostic-fidelity issue worth filing.

## Three actions needed

1. **Immediate: restore bujn-winpc's sshd_config to a working state** before anything else touches that file. The running sshd is fine right now, but the on-disk config is a time bomb. I can revert by either:
   - Reading the chunkA_stdout content from line 57 onward (drop the REPL transcript) and writing back, OR
   - Telling the operator to overwrite from a known-good baseline

2. **Fix the bug in caparison-windows Phase 1.** The Match block append should NOT re-encode chunkA_stdout. Instead, after chunk-A completes, issue a separate non-interactive `Get-Content -Raw` via `zbujb_admin_powershell` to capture clean bytes, then do the strip+append+encode+write cycle on those. This separates "chunk-A as password-fallback session that runs the script" from "phase 1 Match block step that needs clean file bytes."

3. **Mint a WSG rule (or bug-file) about interactive-PowerShell stdout contamination.** This is exactly the kind of body-author footgun that WSp-109 catches at a different layer. The new rule would say something like: "Heredoc-fed interactive PowerShell sessions echo every input line; their stdout cannot be treated as pure-data output of the trailing command. Use non-interactive `powershell -Command` for any operation whose stdout will be consumed as data."

The scope here is wider than I expected. Want me to:
- (a) Just revert sshd_config to a clean state and stop, so we can discuss the full slice cleanly?
- (b) Revert sshd_config AND propose the chunkA fix as a code edit?
- (c) Something else?

The current notch (9dc7fd02) is sound — it landed real fixes (WSp-109, helper escape, 3 invigilate facts). The new bug is pre-existing under a69f25fb and would have shown up the first time anyone re-ran caparison-windows on a host where Phase 1 needed to append a Match block.

lastPrompt: update WSG/BSG, add facts, then run caparison.

file.content:
# zbujb_caparison_windows_exec_with_password_fallback STDOUT_FILE STDERR_FILE
# Reads a PowerShell script from stdin and runs it on the remote node as
# the privileged user. publickey,password preferred (BatchMode=no allows
# /dev/tty password prompt on first run). Default Windows OpenSSH shell is
# cmd.exe; we explicitly invoke `powershell -NoProfile -File -` to feed
# the script via stdin. Returns ssh's exit code (caller decides).
zbujb_caparison_windows_exec_with_password_fallback() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  local -r z_stdout_file="${1:-}"
  local -r z_stderr_file="${2:-}"
  test -n "${z_stdout_file}" || buc_die "zbujb_caparison_windows_exec_with_password_fallback: stdout_file required"
  test -n "${z_stderr_file}" || buc_die "zbujb_caparison_windows_exec_with_password_fallback: stderr_file required"

  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"           \
      "${ZBUJB_SSH_BASE_ARGS[@]}"                \
      -o BatchMode=no                            \
      -o PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password \
      -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_connecttimeout_15}"     \
      "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}"     \
      "${BUJB_ps_invoke_file_stdin}"             \
      > "${z_stdout_file}"                       \
      2> "${z_stderr_file}"
}

# zbujb_caparison_windows_exec_keyonly STDOUT_FILE STDERR_FILE
# Same as above but BatchMode=yes (no password fallback). Used for phase 2.
zbujb_caparison_windows_exec_keyonly() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  local -r z_stdout_file="${1:-}"
  local -r z_stderr_file="${2:-}"
  test -n "${z_stdout_file}" || buc_die "zbujb_caparison_windows_exec_keyonly: stdout_file required"
  test -n "${z_stderr_file}" || buc_die "zbujb_caparison_windows_exec_keyonly: stderr_file required"

  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"           \
      "${ZBUJB_SSH_BASE_ARGS[@]}"                \
      -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_batchmode_yes}"         \
      -o PreferredAuthentications=publickey      \
      -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_connecttimeout_15}"     \
      "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}"     \
      "${BUJB_ps_invoke_file_stdin}"             \
      > "${z_stdout_file}"                       \
      2> "${z_stderr_file}"
}

# zbujb_caparison_windows_verify_directives REMOTE_FILE -- load REMOTE_FILE (raw
# sshd_config bytes from PowerShell Get-Content), strip CR, then assert
# each directive in BUJB_sshd_hardening appears with the expected value.
# Load-then-iterate (no nested while-read on stdin); pure parameter
# expansion + case (no awk/grep/tr).
zbujb_caparison_windows_verify_directives() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  local -r z_remote_file="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_remote_file}" || buc_die "zbujb_caparison_windows_verify_directives: remote_file required"
  test -f "${z_remote_file}" || buc_die "zbujb_caparison_windows_verify_directives: remote_file not found: ${z_remote_file}"

  local -r z_raw_bytes=$(<"${z_remote_file}")
  test -n "${z_raw_bytes}" || buc_die "zbujb_caparison_windows_verify_directives: empty remote sshd_config bytes: ${z_remote_file}"
  local -r z_clean_bytes="${z_raw_bytes//$'\r'/}"

  local z_directives_roll=()

content.text:
You're right. This function and the chunk-A pattern it serves are textbook antipatterns against both WSG and BCG. Here's what's wrong, anchored to specific rules:

## What `zbujb_caparison_windows_exec_with_password_fallback` actually does

It opens a `BatchMode=no`, password-fallback-permitting SSH session, launches `powershell -NoProfile -File -` on the remote, and the caller heredoc-feeds a multi-line PowerShell script via SSH's stdin to that PS process. Two callers (chunk-A and chunk-B). The chunk-A heredoc is ~50 lines doing five distinct operations:

1. Probe + conditional admin-pubkey install (Test-Path + Add-Content)
2. icacls lockdown on admin authkeys
3. **Sophisticated parsing**: foreach line in sshd_config, regex-match against three directives, build new output list, drop subsequent duplicates, append absent directives, Set-Content back
4. Run sshd -t
5. Get-Content -Raw to emit final bytes for bash-side parse

## How this violates WSG

**WSp-105 (PowerShell bodies are single expressions)** — chunk-A is explicitly the ❌ pattern from WSG's own worked example: "Compound state machine in PS body." Five distinct operations, multiple cross-statement variables (`$pubkey`, `$adminAuthKeys`, `$sshConfig`, `$directives`, `$lines`, `$out`, `$seen`, `$matched`), nested foreach loops, regex parsing, list construction. WSp-105's ✅ form is "Bash-orchestrated single-expression calls" — exactly the opposite of what's here.

**WSs-101 (Script via stdin)** — the rule names the bash form (`bash -s`, heredoc-to-bash) and the failure mode (FD-0 contention; child processes that read stdin eat script bytes prematurely). The PowerShell `-File -` form is the same mechanism with the same hazard class. We just empirically hit a hazard within that class — the interactive-PS REPL bleed surfaced because the PS process running from stdin was emitting prompt + input echo into stdout. That specific symptom may or may not be the canonical `-File -` behavior (the host setup may matter), but the broader point stands: stdin-fed scripts have transport-stack failure modes the WSG rules family was minted to prevent.

**Capture-Decide-Dispatch inversion** — the WSG's framing is "Bash owns the state machine; the remote-side body is one effect." Chunk-A inverts this completely: PowerShell owns the state machine for the SSH-trust posture install; bash just orchestrates the single SSH call and consumes the stdout. This is the design shape WSG exists to prevent.

## How this violates BCG

**BCG line 502 area** bans heredocs anywhere. **WSs-102** extends BCG's bans across the transport boundary. The chunk-A is the heredoc-fed-script pattern — directly forbidden. The 50-line PS script is sophisticated enough that we've forgotten to apply rules at the cmdlet level (the directive-merge regex `^\s*#*\s*$k\s+\S` is doing PS-side parsing of file content; the foreach builds state; the final Set-Content commits). All of this should be bash logic.

## The "sophisticated parsing" framing is exactly right

The directive-merge logic in PS lines 32-49 of chunk-A (foreach line, regex check, conditional add to output list, dedupe tracking via `$seen`) is **algorithmic transformation of file content inside a PS body**. That's the WSp-105 ❌ pattern at its sharpest. The same logic in bash would be: capture sshd_config bytes, run a load-then-iterate pass over the lines with case/parameter expansion, build the new content as a bash string, write back via one `WriteAllBytes` call. Each PS round-trip is one cmdlet with simple args.

## What the decomposed shape would look like

Caparison-Windows Phase 1 as atomic single-cmdlet calls, all key-only:

1. `zbujb_powershell_capture` → `Get-Content $env:ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config -Raw` → clean bytes (or fail loud — WSp-109 escape applies, but body has only `'`-quoted paths so no inner `"`)
2. Bash builds the new content: existing directive replacement + Match block append, using parameter expansion + case + a load-then-iterate over lines. Single source of truth.
3. `zbujb_admin_powershell` → `[System.IO.File]::WriteAllBytes(...)` with base64-encoded new bytes. Already the shape the post-chunkA Match block step uses.
4. `zbujb_admin_powershell` → `& 'C:\Windows\System32\OpenSSH\sshd.exe' -t`. Single cmdlet.
5. Probe admin pubkey presence: `zbujb_powershell_capture` → `Get-Content administrators_authorized_keys -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue`. Bash decides if already present.
6. If absent: `zbujb_admin_powershell` → `Add-Content -Path ... -Value ... -Encoding ASCII`.
7. `zbujb_admin_powershell` → icacls one-liner. Already a single binary.
8. Chunk-B Restart-Service: `zbujb_admin_powershell` → `Restart-Service sshd`. Disconnect-expected.

Every step is one PS cmdlet or one native binary per body — WSp-105 clean. Zero heredocs. Zero stdin-feed. Zero `-File -` invocation. Zero password-fallback session.

## What about first-run password fallback?

This is the only design question. Today caparison's first-run-on-fresh-host story is: "operator's manual password gets us key auth working as part of caparison Phase 1's chunk-A." That convenience is what justifies the password-fallback shape.

The cost is real: the password-fallback transport variant has carried this entire compound-PS-body design with it. The "BatchMode=no + multi-cmdlet heredoc" combo is what enables the convenience.

Two alternatives that fit WSG/BCG:

(a) **Move first-run admin trust to BUSJHW handbook scope.** Operator places the admin pubkey out-of-band on first-run-ever (Windows-side equivalent of ssh-copy-id — already partially handbook scope for Linux/Mac per the paddock). Then caparison-windows presumes key auth and uses zbujb_admin_powershell exclusively. The two exec helpers disappear; chunk-A decomposes into single cmdlets; the entire `-File -` invocation pattern disappears from caparison. Cost: one extra handbook step on first-run-ever per machine. Today this scope already exists in BUSJHW (line 256 of paddock: "Operator-manual Windows scope: install OpenSSH server, enable the service, set `PasswordAuthentication yes` temporarily, allow the firewall port, ensure the admin user has a known password..."). Adding "place admin pubkey via PowerShell one-liner the operator runs locally" is one item in that list, not a phase change.

(b) **Keep password fallback but isolate it to a single SSH call.** A new lightweight verb (or chunk-A predecessor) does ONLY the admin pubkey install via password-fallback session. After that returns 0, the rest of caparison uses key-only zbujb_admin_powershell. Cost: still have one transport variant with a heredoc, but it's tiny (one cmdlet equivalent: Add-Content) and bounded to first-run-only.

(a) is the cleaner answer per "load-bearing complexity" — the password-fallback transport is non-load-bearing once first-run is handbook-scope. (b) is a smaller blast radius if you want to keep the convenience.

## Where this leaves us

The current notch (9dc7fd02) landed real fixes — WSp-109 + helper escape + 3 invigilate facts. None of that work is wasted; it stands on its own and is correct under either alternative above.

The chunk-A decomposition is a bigger pace shape than I was implicitly assuming. It probably wants its own pace (still under ₣A-, but a follow-on to ₢A-ABG rather than absorbed into it) because:
- Decomposing chunk-A touches sshd-hardening logic that has bash-side implications (BUSJCW Phase 1 step shape changes)
- First-run handbook scope shifts BUSJHW content
- The decision between (a) and (b) is design-level, not mechanical

And **right now bujn-winpc has a malformed sshd_config on disk** from the failed caparison run. The running sshd is fine because the service didn't restart, but a reboot or manual Restart-Service will brick admin SSH. That needs reverting before anything else.

Want me to:
1. Revert bujn-winpc's sshd_config (manually via privileged SSH, single Remove-Item + restore from backup, or write back the clean tail of chunkA_stdout)?
2. Stop, and chat through (a) vs (b) before any more code?

Or do you want to redirect entirely?

lastPrompt: Whoa.  We definitely need to back up and chat this out, I don't understand.  I think I let you go too far in designing and coding, so now I'm disconnected.  zbujb_caparison_windows_exec_with_password_…

aiTitle: Field exercise Windows SSH garrison setup convergence

message.content: "(b) Keep password fallback" seems by far the simplest.  Is that decision enough to explode the rest of caparison into simpler noninteractive invocations?

content.text:
Yes — choosing (b) collapses most of caparison-windows. Here's the cascade.

## What option (b) reduces to

A single password-fallback SSH session whose entire purpose is **one operation**: add the admin pubkey to `administrators_authorized_keys` idempotently. That's it. Everything else in caparison-windows runs over the existing key-only single-cmdlet helpers (`zbujb_admin_powershell` + `zbujb_powershell_capture`).

## What that does to chunk-A

Chunk-A today bundles five operations behind one heredoc-fed `-File -` session. Each one decomposes:

| Today (chunk-A line) | After (b) |
|---|---|
| Admin pubkey install (Test-Path + Add-Content) | The ONLY operation in the password-fallback session. Body shrinks from ~50 lines to ~3. |
| icacls admin authkeys lockdown | One `zbujb_admin_powershell` call — `icacls` is already a single binary. Key-only (the password-fallback session just installed the pubkey, so key auth works). |
| sshd_config 3-directive harden ("sophisticated parsing") | CDD pattern: `zbujb_powershell_capture` → `Get-Content sshd_config -Raw`; bash does the directive-merge using the same load-then-iterate pattern already proven for the Match block append; `zbujb_admin_powershell` → `[System.IO.File]::WriteAllBytes(base64-of-new-bytes)`. Three single-cmdlet calls + bash logic in the middle. |
| sshd -t | One `zbujb_admin_powershell` call — `& 'sshd.exe' -t` is a single binary invocation. |
| Get-Content -Raw (emit bytes for bash parse) | Disappears. The CDD probe above already captures clean bytes; no separate emit needed. |

## What disappears entirely

**`zbujb_caparison_windows_exec_keyonly`** — gone. Its only caller is the Phase 2 reconnect, which today heredoc-feeds `Get-Content sshd_config -Raw` to get bytes for the directive-verify. That collapses to a single `zbujb_powershell_capture` call. The function isn't replaced; it's deleted.

**Chunk-B's password-fallback transport** — gone. Today chunk-B (Restart-Service) uses `exec_with_password_fallback` defensively. After (b), by chunk-B time the admin pubkey is in place and key auth works; Restart-Service is one `zbujb_admin_powershell` call with disconnect-expected semantics.

**The `BUJB_ps_invoke_file_stdin` constant + the entire `-File -` invocation pattern** — gone from caparison's call surface. Caparison-windows uses only `powershell -NoProfile -Command "..."` going forward.

## What remains compound

The password-fallback session itself still contains a tiny probe-then-act inside one PS body (Test-Path + Get-Content + conditional Add-Content), because bash can't orchestrate across it — we'd be re-prompting the operator for password on each round-trip. That's a bounded WSp-105 exception with a clearly stated justification (single-password-prompt UX), one place, one operation, ~3 PS expressions, no parsing, no algorithmic transformation. The WSG carve-out shape parallels the nuclear-cleanup absent-state collapse exception WSp-108 already carries — same justification structure (load-bearing constraint + bounded scope + minimal compound shape).

If you want to push further: the password-fallback session could shrink to **one** unconditional `Add-Content` cmdlet (one-line body, WSp-105 strictly clean), with dedupe handled by a follow-up key-only session. Cost: caparison re-runs grow the file by one duplicate each time, and a key-only dedupe pass runs immediately after. Either choice is fine; the strict-WSp-105 form is one extra session per caparison invocation.

## Transport variant count

| | Before | After (b) |
|---|---|---|
| Key-only single-cmdlet | `zbujb_admin_powershell` + `zbujb_powershell_capture` (2 fns, used widely) | Same. Used everywhere. |
| Heredoc-fed `-File -` with password fallback | `exec_with_password_fallback` (used by chunk-A + chunk-B) | One tiny call: admin pubkey install only. |
| Heredoc-fed `-File -` key-only | `exec_keyonly` (Phase 2 reconnect) | Gone. |

Three transport variants → effectively two, with the second one bounded to a single named operation.

## BUSJCW spec implications

The spec today describes Phase 1 as one chunk-A bundle + one chunk-B bundle + the recent Match block extension. Under (b), Phase 1 becomes a sequence of ~10 single-cmdlet steps (axhos_steps under the orchestration-style discipline), each carrying its own `{busc_call}` + `{busc_fatal}` boundary. The chunk-A / chunk-B bundling vocabulary drops out of the spec; the Match block append step (which is already shaped as discrete axhos_steps) becomes the structural template the directive-merge step copies.

## Recommendation

**Yes, (b) is enough.** The decomposition is mechanical once the design decision lands. The cascade is large because chunk-A was carrying ~80% of caparison's transport-variant complexity, and removing it ripples through the spec, the helpers, and the Phase 2 reconnect.

Want me to:
1. First: revert bujn-winpc's sshd_config (the time bomb sitting on disk right now)?
2. Then: shape this as a follow-on pace docket under ₣A- — chunk-A decomposition + BUSJCW spec collapse + helper deletion + (the question of dedupe-via-second-session vs bounded compound body)?

The current pace (₢A-ABG) closes when this decomposition lands; without it the field exercise can't complete.

attachment.snippet:
1	# WSG — Windows Scripting Guide
2	
3	## Purpose
4	
5	WSG codifies the discipline for embedding remote shell actions (PowerShell or
6	bash) inside ssh sessions to Windows hosts, with full error trapping at every
7	layer. WSG extends BCG's bash discipline into the ssh-to-Windows transport
8	stack — where curia bash code authors a string that traverses ssh → Windows
9	OpenSSH → cmd.exe → child shell → script execution.
10	
11	## Core Philosophy
12	
13	**Every layer is a fault domain.** A character that means one thing in
14	curia bash means another in cmd.exe, another in the Windows argv parser,
15	another in PowerShell's `-Command` parser, and another in Linux bash. The
16	transport transforms the body at each boundary; errors at any layer can be
17	silently swallowed if not trapped.
18	
19	**The cmd.exe layer is invisible from curia.** Every command is wrapped in
20	`cmd.exe /c <command>` before any other shell sees it. Cmd.exe's quoting
21	rules (`"..."` only; no single-quote support; idiosyncratic `\` handling)
22	bite bodies that look bash-correct from the curia.
23	
24	**Some object output formatters are lazy.** PowerShell flushes string
25	output eagerly but some cmdlet formatter cycles flush only on full
26	pipeline completion; `exit` mid-body discards unflushed output. Treat the
27	lazy default as the safe assumption (WSp-102).
28	
29	**Trap, don't trust.** Assume any error which can plausibly be silenced
30	WILL be silenced if not trapped. Surface every non-zero exit, every empty
31	stdout, every unflushed buffer.
32	
33	## Transport Stack
34	
35	A typical chain:
36	
37	```
38	1. Local bash (curia)            builds ssh argument string
39	2. ssh client                    forwards command to remote
40	3. Windows OpenSSH sshd          spawns DefaultShell (cmd.exe)
41	4. cmd.exe /c <command>          parses tokens, respects "...", spawns child
42	5. Child shell (one of):
43	   a. powershell.exe -Command "<body>"     runs body in PowerShell
44	   b. wsl.exe ... bash -c "<body>"          forwards to Linux bash in WSL
45	   c. C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -c "..." runs Cygwin bash
46	6. Body subprocess(es)           child processes inherit parent's FD 0
47	```
48	
49	Each step has its own quoting model. Each can fail silently.
50	
51	## Established Rules
52	
53	Headers tag the rule's *named* phenomenon: ❌ for a failure mode, ✅ for an
54	established correct shape. `WSp-` rules constrain PowerShell body
55	authoring; `WSs-` rules constrain shell/bash body authoring; `WSt-` rules
56	constrain transport quoting, escaping, and exit-code propagation across
57	boundaries (any body language). Numbering starts at 101 within each
58	family to leave room for insertions without renumbering.
59	
60	### ❌ WSp-101: Trailing `exit $LASTEXITCODE` without LASTEXITCODE init
61	
62	In a fresh `powershell -Command` with no native command, `$LASTEXITCODE`
63	is `$null`; the typed comparison `$null -ne 0` evaluates `True`, so the
64	trailer fires unconditionally and `exit $null` short-circuits the lazy
65	object-formatter pipeline, discarding buffered cmdlet output. Initialize
66	`$LASTEXITCODE = 0` so the trailer only fires on real native-command
67	failures.
68	
69	```powershell
70	# ❌ Eats Get-LocalUser's table output (returns CRLF only)
71	powershell -Command "Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue;
72	                     if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }"
73	
74	# ✅ Initialize so the branch only fires on real native command failures
75	powershell -Command "$LASTEXITCODE = 0;
76	                     Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue;
77	                     if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }"
78	```
79	
80	### ❌ WSp-102: `exit` mid-body before object formatters flush
81	
82	PowerShell flushes string output eagerly but some cmdlet object outputs
83	lazily (Out-Default → Format-Table → render); `exit` aborts the formatter
84	pipeline before lazy outputs render. Assume any cmdlet output is lazy
85	unless verified otherwise; materialize with `| Out-String` before any
86	exit. WSp-101's `$LASTEXITCODE = 0` makes this moot for happy paths;
87	failure paths still need string-materialization.
88	
89	```powershell
90	# ❌ Get-LocalUser's table is lost; Get-Date's string survives
91	powershell -Command "Get-Date; Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo'; exit 0"
92	
93	# ✅ Materialize objects to strings before any potential exit
94	powershell -Command "Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' | Out-String; ..."
95	```
96	
97	### ✅ WSp-103: PowerShell single-quoted strings survive nesting
98	
99	Cmd.exe treats single quotes as literal characters; PowerShell recognizes
100	`'...'` as a string literal. Use single quotes for PS literals inside the
101	outer `"..."` — they nest reliably through every layer.
102	
103	```bash
104	# ✅ Single-quoted PS literals inside outer "..."
105	ssh ... "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"Get-LocalUser -Name 'username'\""
106	```
107	
108	### ✅ WSp-104: WSp-102's lazy-flush behavior is transport-agnostic
109	
110	Lazy-flush is a property of the body, not of how PowerShell was launched —
111	the same `Get-LocalUser; exit 0` body emits empty stdout through every
112	transport. The fix (Out-String materialization, or WSp-101's `$LASTEXITCODE = 0`)
113	applies uniformly.
114	
115	### ❌ WSp-105: PowerShell bodies are single expressions
116	
117	A PowerShell body (`powershell -Command "<body>"` or capture variant)
118	MUST be exactly one of:
119	
120	1. One cmdlet call (arguments, parameters, pipelined formatters like
121	   `| Out-String` or `| Out-Null`).
122	2. One native binary call.
123	3. One native binary with an inner-shell body
124	   (e.g. `wsl.exe ... bash -c "..."`).
125	
126	The enumeration is constructive. Forbidden at body top level: `if`,
127	`try`/`catch`, `foreach`, `while`, `switch`, `do`, `&&`/`||`,
128	`;`-separated statements, intermediate `$var` assignments, ternary `?:`.
129	A single `if`-guarded effect like
130	`if (Test-Path '...') { Remove-Item '...' }` is also forbidden — the
131	body decides AND acts; decisions belong in bash. See CDD below.
132	
133	The wrapper-side prelude
134	(`$ErrorActionPreference='Stop'; $env:WSL_UTF8=1; $LASTEXITCODE=0; <body>; if ...`)
135	is library code; the rule constrains caller bodies only. The wrapper's
136	exact three-slot shape (prelude / body / postlude) is the minimum-viable
137	scaffolding given WSp-101, WSp-102, WSp-105, and WSp-109 acting in
138	combination — see
139	`Memos/memo-20260511-windows-transport-wrapper-postlude-synthesis.md`
140	for the integrative rationale and the elimination argument showing why
141	each token is load-bearing.
142	
143	```bash
144	# ❌ Compound state machine in PS body
145	priv_ps "\$sid=(Get-LocalUser '${z_user}').SID.Value; \$path='HKLM:\\...\\' + \$sid; New-Item \$path -Force | Out-Null; New-ItemProperty \$path -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"
146	
147	# ❌ Single `if`-guard — body decides AND acts; state machine belongs in bash
148	priv_ps "if (Test-Path '${z_tar_path}') { Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}' }"
149	
150	# ✅ Bash-orchestrated single-expression calls
151	z_sid=$(ps_capture "(Get-LocalUser '${z_user}').SID.Value") || die "..."
152	z_regkey="HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\ProfileList\\${z_sid}"
153	priv_ps "New-Item -Path '${z_regkey}' -Force | Out-Null"
154	priv_ps "New-ItemProperty -Path '${z_regkey}' -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"
155	
156	# ✅ Single guarded effect repaired via Capture-Decide-Dispatch
157	z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_tar_path}'") || die "..."
158	if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
159	  priv_ps "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}'" || die "..."
160	fi
161	```
162	
163	### ❌ WSp-106: Don't interpolate strings via PowerShell when bash can build them
164	
165	The bash caller builds the string by expanding `${bash_var}` before
166	sending; the PS body receives a literal. Bash escape rules cascade once;
167	adding PS-side interpolation (`"...$var..."`) layers another substitution
168	model on top and compounds with WSp-107's native-binary quoting traps.
169	
170	```bash
171	# ❌ PS-side string concatenation

... [551 lines truncated] ...

content.text:
bujn-winpc's `sshd_config` reverted to clean post-directive-merge content (the salvageable bytes from chunkA_stdout lines 57-148). `sshd -t` validates green; head/tail readback confirms no PS transcript bloat, no rogue Match block. Running sshd still on the OLD config in memory (Restart-Service never happened); the on-disk file is now a valid forward-progress state.

## Plan for chunk-A decomposition (option (b) cascade)

**Scope:** caparison-windows Phase 1 and Phase 2 reconnect.

**Helper layer changes:**
- **New:** one small password-fallback wrapper that runs ONE PS expression — admin pubkey install only. Replaces the multi-line heredoc transport.
- **Delete:** `zbujb_caparison_windows_exec_keyonly` (only consumer is Phase 2 reconnect, which becomes one `zbujb_powershell_capture` call).
- **Delete:** `zbujb_caparison_windows_exec_with_password_fallback` (chunk-A and chunk-B callers go away).
- **Delete:** `BUJB_ps_invoke_file_stdin` constant + the entire `-File -` invocation pattern.
- **Keep, no change:** `zbujb_admin_powershell`, `zbujb_powershell_capture` — bear the entire caparison load.

**Phase 1 step decomposition** (today's chunk-A + post-chunk-A Match block + chunk-B → ~13 single-cmdlet steps):

| # | Op | Transport |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Install admin pubkey idempotently | password-fallback session (the only one) |
| 2 | icacls lockdown on admin authkeys | key-only `zbujb_admin_powershell` |
| 3 | Capture sshd_config bytes | key-only `zbujb_powershell_capture` `Get-Content -Raw` |
| 4 | Bash: directive merge (3 hardened directives) | bash-side load-then-iterate, mirror of the existing Match block strip pattern |
| 5 | Bash: Match block strip + append | existing bash pattern, unchanged |
| 6 | Bash: base64-encode the new bytes | bash |
| 7 | Write new sshd_config | key-only `zbujb_admin_powershell` `[System.IO.File]::WriteAllBytes` |
| 8 | sshd -t validate | key-only `zbujb_admin_powershell` |
| 9 | Probe workload authkeys directory presence | key-only `zbujb_powershell_capture` `Test-Path` |
| 10 | Conditional create workload authkeys directory | key-only `zbujb_admin_powershell` `New-Item` |
| 11 | icacls workload authkeys directory | key-only `zbujb_admin_powershell` |
| 12 | Match block re-verify via existing helper | key-only `zbujb_powershell_capture` `Get-Content -Raw` + existing `verify_match_block` |
| 13 | Restart-Service sshd | key-only `zbujb_admin_powershell` (disconnect-expected) |

**Phase 2 reconnect:** today's heredoc-fed `-File -` keyonly + verify_directives → one `zbujb_powershell_capture` `Get-Content -Raw` + existing `verify_directives` bash parse. No exec helper needed.

**Phase 3 (post-trust admin posture):** already single-cmdlet `zbujb_admin_powershell` calls throughout. Unchanged.

**BUSJCW spec changes:** Phase 1 spec rewritten as 13 axhos_steps under the orchestration-style discipline (parallel to the BUSJGW restructure that 461c65aa minted). "Chunk A / chunk B" vocabulary drops. Each step carries `{busc_call}` + `{busc_fatal}` boundary per WSp-105.

**Field validation:** re-run caparison-windows on bujn-winpc (one operator password prompt for step 1; everything else key-only). Then re-run garrison-w (workload authkeys directory now provisioned; place-bare-trust lands clean; the new invigilate facts gate it).

## Clarifying questions before I touch any of this

**Q1 — Password-fallback session sub-shape.** The session installs admin pubkey idempotently. Two options for the body:

- **(b.1) Bounded compound body:** ~3 PS expressions in one body (Test-Path + Get-Content membership check + conditional Add-Content), with a documented WSp-105 exception parallel to WSp-108's nuclear-cleanup carve-out. One named place, one operation, no parsing, no algorithmic state, justified by single-password-prompt UX.
- **(b.3) Strict WSp-105 with second-session dedupe:** first session does ONE unconditional `Add-Content` cmdlet (single-expression body, WSp-105 strict). Caparison invocations accumulate duplicate lines in `administrators_authorized_keys` over time. Optional follow-up key-only session in the same caparison run does Get-Content + bash-side dedupe + Set-Content to clean up.

(b.1) is simpler with one tiny documented exception. (b.3) is strictly clean but trades the exception for an extra SSH call and an "always add then sometimes dedupe" pattern.

**Q2 — Restart-Service auth.** Today chunk-B (Restart-Service) uses the password-fallback transport defensively. My read: by Restart-Service time, the admin pubkey is installed (step 1) and sshd reads `administrators_authorized_keys` on every connection — so key auth works regardless of what the OLD config's `PasswordAuthentication` setting was. Under (b), can Restart-Service be key-only? Or do you want the password-fallback retained there for first-run safety?

**Q3 — Pace scope.** This decomposition is sizable (helper deletions + spec rewrite + ~13 axhos_steps + bash callsite restructure). Two scoping options:
- **Absorb into ₢A-ABG.** The pace's "Done when" clause names "Windows setup converged" and the decomposition is required for that convergence (chunk-A's REPL bleed IS observed Windows OpenSSH behavior the spec/code is incoherent against).
- **Spin a new pace immediately after ₢A-ABG.** ₢A-ABG closes with today's fixes + the reversion + paddock annotation surfacing the chunk-A finding. New pace (`caparison-phase1-decompose` or similar) takes the decomposition as a focused slice.

My recommendation: spin a new pace. The decomposition has its own coherent shape (helper layer + Phase 1 spec rewrite + field re-test); it's a clean follow-on rather than fold-in.

**Q4 — Phase 1 spec granularity.** 13 axhos_steps is the maximal expansion. Some could group naturally (e.g., steps 3-7 as "sshd_config rewrite sub-phase"). The orchestration-style discipline (461c65aa exemplar) leans toward fine granularity. Stay 13-step, or apply some grouping?

Awaiting answers before any further edits.

content.text:
## Answers

**Q1 — WSp exemption check: none covers bounded compound bodies.** WSp-105 strictly forbids the `Test-Path + if + Add-Content` shape (it's explicitly the ❌ "Single `if`-guard — body decides AND acts" worked example). WSp-108's nuclear-cleanup carve-out is dispatch-side ErrorAction suppression, different domain. Per your instruction: doing it simple. Password-fallback session = **one cmdlet only** (`Add-Content`).

The simple form has a clean structural property: **gate the password-fallback session behind a key-auth probe at the very start of caparison**. CDD shape:

```
probe: zbujb_admin_powershell 'exit 0' (key-only)
  → succeeds  → skip the password-fallback session entirely (re-run case)
  → fails     → enter password-fallback session, single Add-Content
```

Net result: first-caparison-ever on a fresh host triggers exactly one password prompt; every re-run is key-only end-to-end; `Add-Content` runs at most once per host's lifetime, so duplicate pubkey accumulation is a non-issue. The compound-body question disappears because the compound is replaced by bash-side CDD orchestration.

**Q2 — Restart-Service: key-only is both simplest AND most robust.** Reasoning:

- By Restart-Service time, the admin pubkey install (step 1) succeeded — otherwise caparison would have already died.
- sshd reads `administrators_authorized_keys` on every connection (no caching), so key auth works against the running OLD sshd regardless of what its `PasswordAuthentication` setting was.
- The OLD sshd's `PasswordAuthentication` state only matters if the admin pubkey couldn't be installed — and that branch already died before reaching Restart-Service.
- Adding password-fallback at Restart-Service guards against a failure mode that can't occur. Defense against a non-existent threat is complexity tax with no payoff.
- Disconnect-on-success is unchanged; one key-only single-cmdlet call replaces the heredoc-fed `-File -` keyonly form.

Recommendation: key-only.

**Q3 — Chivvy a new pace, locking the decisions above.** Drafting silks `caparison-windows-decompose` + docket via gazette below.

**Q4 — WSG says 13 steps.** 13 axhos_steps, no grouping. Mirrors the BUSJGW restructure (461c65aa) exemplar.

Writing the gazette now.

Chivvied. New pace **₢A-ABH** (silks `caparison-windows-decompose`) is now first in ₣A-'s queue, ahead of the in-flight ₢A-ABG. Docket carries the four locked decisions from this chat.

message.content:
Q1) okay lets do that

Q2) what do you mean by key-only?

content.text:
By "key-only" I mean an SSH session authenticated by SSH private key with no possibility of falling back to anything interactive. Concretely:

```bash
ssh -i ${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE} \
    -o BatchMode=yes \
    -o PreferredAuthentications=publickey \
    ${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST} \
    "..."
```

Two SSH options compose the guarantee:

- **`BatchMode=yes`** — SSH never prompts the user for anything. If something interactive would be needed (password, passphrase, host key confirmation), SSH fails immediately rather than touching `/dev/tty`. The bash orchestrator gets back a non-zero exit code, never a hang.
- **`PreferredAuthentications=publickey`** — only the publickey auth method is offered to the server. Password, keyboard-interactive, GSSAPI, etc. are taken off the table at the protocol level, not just hidden behind BatchMode.

Compare with what `zbujb_caparison_windows_exec_with_password_fallback` does today (line 1428-1429):

```bash
-o BatchMode=no
-o PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password
```

That session tries key first; if key fails, it falls back to password, and BatchMode=no allows SSH to read the password from `/dev/tty`. That's the only mechanism in caparison that ever touches `/dev/tty`.

The existing `zbujb_admin_powershell` and `zbujb_powershell_capture` helpers are already key-only by construction — they use `BUJB_ssh_opt_batchmode_yes` (BatchMode=yes) and the implicit key auth from `-i`. The paddock's "Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported" line is what makes BatchMode=yes safe: with unencrypted privkeys, there's no passphrase prompt either.

So in the new shape:
- **One password-fallback SSH session ever per host's lifetime** (admin pubkey install on first caparison), and only because the operator's typed password is the one bootstrap input.
- **Every other SSH session in caparison** uses key-only as defined above — Restart-Service included.

Q1 confirmed: proceeding with key-auth probe → conditional one-cmdlet password-fallback Add-Content → everything else key-only single-cmdlet. Want me to mount ₢A-ABH and start, or chat through anything else first?
